pattern: Deciding, for every subset of the remaining jobs, the best possible finish time when it is handled by a fixed number of workers turns an assignment search into a bitmask dynamic program over subset sums.
hints: Precompute, for every subset of jobs (as a bitmask), the total time those jobs would take on a single worker -- that's just the sum of the jobs in the subset.

Think recursively: given a set of jobs still unassigned and w workers left, try every way of carving off a subset for 'the next worker' and recurse on the rest with w-1 workers; the cost of a choice is the max of that subset's sum and whatever the remaining workers need.

Enumerate only the submasks of the current remaining set (a mask-of-a-mask trick) so you never try the same subset twice, memoize on (remaining set, workers left), and the answer is the value for (all jobs, k workers).

edge_inputs.description: statement example, three jobs three workers
statement example, five jobs two workers
workers equal job count, every job gets its own worker
single worker must absorb every job
equal jobs split evenly across fewer workers than jobs
one dominant job forces the answer regardless of how the rest are split
mixed small values with more workers than strictly needed
maximum job value on a single job among small ones

stress.description: near-maximum job counts with random values and a random worker count
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(8, 12)
    jobs = [rng.randint(1, 10000000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [jobs, k]
stress.note: n capped at 12 to match the bitmask DP's exponential state space
